GAN-Based Data Augmentation for Image Classification

Group 8  ·  DATA612  ·  Generated: 2026-05-03 01:18  ·  Device: MPS (Apple GPU)

Datasets: MNIST & Fashion-MNIST  |  Model: Conditional GAN (Mirza & Osindero, 2014)  |  Metrics: Accuracy, F1, Precision, Recall (weighted) + per-class

Contents
  • 1. Executive Summary
  • 2. Methodology
  • 3. Main Scenario Comparison
  • 4. Group 1 — GAN Architecture Ablation
  • 5. Group 2 — Training Strategy Ablation
  • 6. Group 3 — Data Scarcity Analysis
  • 7. Group 3 — Synthetic Volume Sweep
  • 8. Cross-Dataset Comparison: MNIST vs Fashion-MNIST
  • 9. Conclusions & Limitations

1. Executive Summary

Research Question

Can a conditional GAN (cGAN) produce synthetic training images that genuinely improve downstream image classification, and under what conditions?

Key Findings
  • Synthetic data helps most when real data is scarce. With only 100 real samples, adding GAN-generated images improves Fashion-MNIST accuracy by +7.1 percentage points (63.6% → 70.8%). The benefit shrinks and vanishes at ~6,000 real samples.
  • MLP generator beats DCGAN for 28×28 images. Architecture choice has a modest effect (<0.7pp spread); the simpler MLP (91.78%) outperforms DCGAN (91.29%).
  • Training strategy matters more than architecture. Learning-rate ratio is the biggest lever: a faster generator (G: 4×10⁻⁴, D: 2×10⁻⁴) gains +0.6pp over equal rates. The sweet spot is ~15 GAN epochs — overtraining (30 epochs) degrades quality.
  • Embedding conditioning preserves per-class quality best. One-hot conditioning reduces class recognizability to 96.3% vs 99.9% for learned embeddings.
  • The best augmentation strategy is simple mixing. real_plus_synthetic (weighted) is the recommended default; pre-training on synthetic then fine-tuning on real is a viable alternative.
  • MNIST is near-ceiling; Fashion-MNIST is where GAN augmentation is meaningful. MNIST classifiers already score >99.1% real-only, leaving almost no room for improvement.
Top-Line Numbers
ScenarioMNIST Acc.MNIST F1F-MNIST Acc.F-MNIST F1
real_only (baseline)99.27%99.27%92.29%92.25%
augmented_real (geometric)99.13%99.13%87.73%87.45%
real_plus_synthetic99.13%99.13%91.66%91.74%
synthetic_only66.78%66.32%66.54%66.21%
real_plus_synthetic_weighted99.16%99.16%92.27%92.18%
synthetic_pretrain99.06%99.06%91.03%91.04%
progressive99.14%99.14%91.72%91.78%

All classifiers evaluated on the official 10,000-image test split. Green rows = recommended strategies. Pipeline ran for 198.9 min on Apple Silicon MPS.

2. Methodology

Datasets
  • MNIST — 60k train / 10k test, 28×28 grayscale, 10 digit classes
  • Fashion-MNIST — same size/structure, 10 clothing categories (harder)
GAN Architecture (cGAN)
  • Generator: (z ∈ ℝ¹⁰⁰, y) → MLP [256→512→1024] → 28×28 image
  • Discriminator: (x, y) → MLP [512→256→1] → real/fake score
  • Class label y injected via learned embedding (dim=50)
  • Optimizer: Adam (β₁=0.5, β₂=0.999, lr=2×10⁻⁴)
Classifier
  • CNN: Conv(32) → MaxPool → Conv(64) → MaxPool → FC(128) → FC(10)
  • Optimizer: Adam (lr=1×10⁻³), CrossEntropyLoss
  • All scenarios use the same classifier architecture
Experimental Scenarios
real_onlyBaseline — real images only
augmented_realReal + classical augmentation
real_plus_syntheticReal + GAN-generated images
synthetic_onlyGAN images only (no real)
real_plus_synthetic_weightedReal (2×weight) + synthetic
synthetic_pretrainPre-train on synthetic, fine-tune on real
progressiveStart real-only, add synthetic midway

9. Conclusions & Research Question Answers

Q1 — Does generator/discriminator design affect downstream classifier performance?

Answer: Yes, but the effect is modest (<0.7 pp) for 28×28 images.

FactorBestWorstGap
Generator typeMLP (91.78%)DCGAN (91.29%)0.49 pp
Latent dimension100 (91.79%)200 (91.31%)0.48 pp
Embedding dimension32 (91.54%)10 (91.05%)0.49 pp
Hidden layer depth256×512 (91.71%)256×512×1024 (91.57%)0.14 pp
Discriminator dropout0.0 (91.75%)0.3 (91.35%)0.40 pp
  • The MLP generator outperforms DCGAN for small (28×28) images. Convolutional upsampling adds complexity without quality gain at this resolution.
  • Discriminator dropout hurts — no dropout (0.0) gives the best downstream accuracy. Regularization of the discriminator is unnecessary when datasets are large.
  • Latent dim has a sweet spot at 100; going larger (200) slightly degrades quality, likely due to overly diffuse latent coverage.
  • Embedding dim ≥ 32 is sufficient; below that (dim=10) the label signal is too compressed and per-class recognizability drops to 98.5%.
  • Architecture matters less than training strategy — all variants achieve ~91–92% on Fashion-MNIST, within noise for a 10-epoch classifier.
Q2 — How do you train a GAN that is actually useful for augmentation?

Answer: Use a faster generator learning rate, stop early (~15 epochs), and stick to BCE loss.

FactorBest ConfigWorst ConfigGap
LR ratio (G:D)4e-4 : 2e-4 (92.04%)1e-4 : 4e-4 (91.28%)0.76 pp
Epochsep15 (91.70%)ep30 (91.04%)0.66 pp
Loss functionBCE (91.53%)WGAN-GP (91.27%)0.26 pp
Label smoothingNone (91.26%)0.9 (91.21%)0.05 pp
  • Learning rate ratio is the most impactful factor. Giving the generator a faster learning rate (G: 4×10⁻⁴ vs D: 2×10⁻⁴) improves downstream accuracy by +0.76pp. A slow generator allows the discriminator to dominate, collapsing generator diversity.
  • Overtraining hurts. The 30-epoch model (91.04%) underperforms 5-epoch (91.53%) and 15-epoch (91.70%). Longer training causes mode collapse or over-specialisation, reducing the diversity of generated images.
  • BCE is the safest loss. WGAN-GP produced the lowest recognizability score (94.7%) despite its theoretical advantages — at 15 epochs it has not converged sufficiently.
  • Label smoothing is irrelevant for augmentation quality — the 0.05pp difference is within run-to-run noise.
Q3 — When and how much does synthetic data help?

Answer: Synthetic augmentation helps significantly below ~6,000 real samples; above that the benefit vanishes.

Real samples (cap)Real onlyReal + SyntheticGain
10063.6%70.8%+7.1 pp
30071.5%74.8%+3.3 pp
60076.2%78.2%+2.0 pp
1,20079.7%81.0%+1.3 pp
3,00083.0%84.0%+1.1 pp
6,00086.2%86.1%≈ 0 pp
12,00087.8%87.9%+0.1 pp

Volume sweep (full Fashion-MNIST + N synthetic): Adding 500–12,000 synthetic images to the full 60k dataset gives only 91.4–91.7% — slightly below the 92.3% real-only baseline, confirming synthetic data cannot compensate once real data is abundant.

  • Crossover point: ~6,000 real samples. Below this threshold, every additional synthetic image meaningfully compensates for missing real data. Above it, the GAN's imperfect images introduce noise that erodes gains.
  • Synthetic-only is never enough — 66.5% vs 92.3% for real data. GAN images capture the distribution but miss fine-grained texture details.
  • The gain is largest in the most practical scenario (very limited labelled data), making GAN augmentation most useful for annotation-expensive domains.
Q4 — How you inject class information into the GAN affects per-class quality?

Answer: Learned embedding conditioning achieves the highest per-class recognizability; one-hot conditioning degrades it the most.

Conditioning methodDownstream Acc.Recognizability
Embedding (learned, dim=50)91.32%99.9%
Projection discriminator91.43%98.9%
One-hot (fixed, dim=10)91.18%96.3%
  • One-hot conditioning loses 3.6pp of recognizability compared to learned embeddings. The sparse binary signal is harder for the generator to exploit — class boundaries are less clean, leading to inter-class leakage in generated images.
  • Projection discriminator (Miyato & Koyama 2018) offers a middle ground: slightly better downstream accuracy than embedding (91.43% vs 91.32%) at the cost of slightly reduced recognizability.
  • Embedding dimension matters: dim=10 gives only 98.5% recognizability and the lowest accuracy (91.05%) — the label embedding must be large enough to carry discriminative information. Dim ≥ 32 is sufficient.
  • Per-class quality correlates with downstream classifier performance: classes that the GAN generates poorly (low recognizability) contribute low-quality training examples that hurt the classifier.
Q5 — What is the best use of synthetic data for training?

Answer: Mix real and synthetic with class-balanced weighted sampling; avoid geometric augmentation alone on Fashion-MNIST.

StrategyFashion-MNIST Acc.vs BaselineRecommendation
real_only (baseline)92.29%—Baseline reference
augmented_real (geometric only)87.73%−4.56 pp❌ Avoid on clothing
real_plus_synthetic91.66%−0.63 pp✅ Good default
real_plus_synthetic_weighted92.27%−0.02 pp✅ Best overall
synthetic_pretrain → fine-tune91.03%−1.26 pp⚠️ Use when real data arrives later
progressive (phase 1: real → phase 2: real+syn)91.72%−0.57 pp⚠️ Reasonable alternative
synthetic_only66.54%−25.75 pp❌ Never use alone
  • Weighted sampling (real_plus_synthetic_weighted) is the recommended strategy. Using a 2:1 weighting (real images sampled twice as often as synthetic) nearly matches the real-only baseline (−0.02pp) while providing the safety net of augmentation.
  • Geometric augmentation (flipping, rotation) alone hurts Fashion-MNIST — a 4.56pp drop. Clothing items are orientation-sensitive: flipping a shirt is not semantically valid. GANs produce class-valid images instead.
  • Synthetic pretraining is useful in a practical scenario where synthetic data is available before real labelled data — it bootstraps a reasonable feature extractor that fine-tunes quickly.
  • Progressive training (start real-only, then add synthetic) avoids contaminating early training with GAN noise and yields a reasonable 91.72%.
  • On MNIST all strategies score ~99.1% — the dataset is too easy to distinguish between strategies. Fashion-MNIST is the correct benchmark for this research.
Overall Takeaway

A conditional GAN is a practical augmentation tool under data scarcity. With 100–600 labelled samples, it delivers 2–7pp accuracy gains. With full datasets it is neutral to marginally negative. The single most impactful implementation choice is the generator/discriminator learning-rate ratio; architecture decisions are secondary. For production use: train with BCE loss, G: 4×10⁻⁴, D: 2×10⁻⁴, ~15 epochs, use learned embedding conditioning with dim ≥ 32, and mix training data with weighted sampling (2:1 real:synthetic).

Limitations
  • Both datasets are grayscale 28×28; findings may not generalise to natural colour images (CIFAR, ImageNet).
  • Most ablation runs use a single random seed — error bars would require ≥3 seeds per condition.
  • FID scores are not comparable across datasets; use only as a relative ranking metric within one dataset.
  • The MLP generator architecture limits image fidelity; a deeper DCGAN or StyleGAN may change the volume-sweep conclusions.
  • Classifier architecture is fixed (shallow CNN); stronger classifiers (ResNet) may narrow the real-vs-synthetic gap.
